coredata.dc:title: Constructing porous polyimide/carbon quantum dots aerogel with efficient photocatalytic property under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Yujie
Yi, Xibin
Li, Bing
Chen, Shiwei
Fan, Zijie
coredata.dc:description: Polyimide was widely studied as semiconductor polymer photocatalyst. However, polyimide photocatalyst suffers from small specific surface area, poor visible light absorption and low charge thansfer efficiency. Herein, porous polyimide/carbon quantum dots aerogel was prepared successfully via supercritical CO2 drying method. The carbon quantum dots were obtained by a green hydrothermal method. The carbon quantum dots could be anchored intimately on the polyimide aerogel by π -π stacking interactions. The polyimide/carbon quantum dots aerogel had large specific surface area and porous structure. The carbon quantum dots increased the visible light absorption, adjusted the band structure of the polyimide aerogel and facilitated the generation and separation of photogenerated electron and holes. Thus the photocatalytic property of the polyimide/carbon quantum dots aerogel was increased compared to pure polyimide aerogel. h+ and ·O2 – were the main active species to degrade the pollutant. Finally, the mechanism of the photocatalysis under the visible light was proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyimide
Carbon quantum dots
Aerogel
Photocatalytic property

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The FTIR spectra of PI, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels.
The XRD spectra of PI, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels.
The SEM images of (a) PI, (b) PI/CQDs-0.5, (c) PI/CQDs-1 and (d) PI/CQDs-3 aerogels.
BET of PI, PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels.
XPS spectra of N 1 s (a) and C 1 s (b) of PI, PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels.
VBXPS(a), Kubelka-Munk plots(b), schematic band structures(c) of PI, PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels.
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of PI, PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels.
Photoluminescence spectra of PI, PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels.
Nyquist plots of PI and PI/CQDs-1 aerogels.

Photocatalytic degradation curves of OTC over PI, PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels(a); Cycle runs for the degradation of OTC over PI/CQDs-1 under visible light (λ > 420 nm)(b)；XRD results of PI/CQDs-1 before and after the recycling photocatalytic tests(c).
Visible-light photocatalytic degradation curves over OTC of PI/CQDs-1 aerogel with and without scavengers.
The photocatalytic mechanism of PI/CQDs aerogel under visible light.

head.ce:title: Constructing porous polyimide/carbon quantum dots aerogel with efficient photocatalytic property under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Processing and Testing Technology of Glass and Functional Ceramics, School of Material Science and Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Processing and Testing Technology of Glass and Functional Ceramics
School of Material Science and Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Processing and Testing Technology of Glass and Functional Ceramics, School of Material Science and Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Key Laboratory for Special Silicon-containing Material, Advanced Materials Institute, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250014, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Key Laboratory for Special Silicon-containing Material
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Key Laboratory for Special Silicon-containing Material, Advanced Materials Institute, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250014, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polyimide was widely studied as semiconductor polymer photocatalyst. However, polyimide photocatalyst suffers from small specific surface area, poor visible light absorption and low charge thansfer efficiency. Herein, porous polyimide/carbon quantum dots aerogel was prepared successfully via supercritical CO2 drying method. The carbon quantum dots were obtained by a green hydrothermal method. The carbon quantum dots could be anchored intimately on the polyimide aerogel by π -π stacking interactions. The polyimide/carbon quantum dots aerogel had large specific surface area and porous structure. The carbon quantum dots increased the visible light absorption, adjusted the band structure of the polyimide aerogel and facilitated the generation and separation of photogenerated electron and holes. Thus the photocatalytic property of the polyimide/carbon quantum dots aerogel was increased compared to pure polyimide aerogel. h+ and ·O2 – were the main active species to degrade the pollutant. Finally, the mechanism of the photocatalysis under the visible light was proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industrial production and technology, environmental pollution becomes more serious [1–4]. Many researchers are actively exploring various effective methods. Photocatalytic technology, which uses the cheap and clean solar energy, is one of the most promising methods and has attracted many researchers’ attention [5–9]. The photocatalyst is the key to the photocatalytic technology [10–15]. Last decades, semiconductor polymer photocatalytic materials were studied widely because of their advantages such as abundant sources, low cost and adjustable structure [16–18]. The usual semiconductor polymer photocatalytic materials include g-C3N4, conjugated microporous polymers, and covalent organic framework [19–28]. Recently, polyimide (PI) photocatalyst was focused on owing to its structure regulation and excellent property [29–31,33–36]. Chu synthesized PI via solid polycondensation and found that the PI could be used as photocatalyst to decompose the water [37]. Chu synthesized PI with different polymerization degrees, which could make UV–vis absorption band red-shift to longer wavelength [38]. Yang prepared O-doped PI photocatalyst and found that electronic structure of PI changed. The absorption of visible light and the separation efficiency of the electron-hole pair enhanced, thus improving its photocatalytic degradation [39]. Wang used S4 as the sulfur atom source to prepare S-doped polyimide and found that S could replace N in the thiotriazinone, which could increase the visible light absorption intensity [32]. Ma used MoO3 to increase the visible light absorption and improve the charge separation efficiency of PI photocatalyst, thus improving its photocatalytic degradation rate over organic dyes [40]. Ma confined monolayer MoS2 quantum dots on conjugated polyimide to speed the charge transfer rate of MoS2/PI. Finally, the photocatalytic property was enhanced significantly compared with that of Pt/PI [41]. However, most photocatalysts above were power, thus their surface area was small.

Polyimide aerogel is a kind of solid material with low density, high porosity and high specific surface area [42,43]. Owing to these advantages, more and more research was done to study polyimide aerogel photocatalyst [44,45].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a new type of carbon material, and its size is less than 10 nm. Since CQDs was discovered in 2004, it has attracted widespread attention [46–49]. The material has the advantages of excellent optical properties, environmental protection, low cost and simple synthesis method [50–53]. The physical and chemical properties of CQDs can be controlled by surface passivation and functionalization. Therefore, CQDs has great application in biosensing, bioimaging, metal ion detection and so on [54–56]. These days, it is found that CQDs has excellent upconversion and semiconductor performance, which makes the CQDs a promising photocatalytic material [57–62]. Zhang prepared CQDs/Ag/Ag3PO4 photocatalytic material and found that CQDs could transfer visible light to short wave of 300–500 nm, thus increasing the efficiency of the visible light [63]. Pan prepared CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic material and found that CQDs could absorb visible light and emit ultraviolet light. Followingly, CQDs / Fe2O3, CQDs/Cu2O, CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs /ZnO photocatalytic materials were prepared. These materials possessed better photocatlytic activity owing to the introduction of CQDs [64].

Oxytetracycline (OTC) is a widely used antibiotic with a large drug dosage in the world. OTC in sewage could greatly affect people's health. Owing to its stable structural stability and recalcitrance to biodegradation, it is difficult to efficiently remove it by traditional water treatment methods. Therefore, studying an effective technology to treat residual OTC in water is very urgent.

Herein, we constructed and prepared the porous PI/CQDs aerogels with large specific surface area. This is the first time to introduce CQDs to the polyimide aerogels. CQDs could effectively increase the light absorption and speed the separation of the photoexcited electron and holes. The aim of the work was to prepare and study the PI/CQDs aerogels with good photocatalytic degradation over OTC under visible light.

3,3ˊ,4,4ˊ-Biphenyltetracarboxylic dianhydride (BPDA), di-(4-aminophenyl)ether (ODA), trimethylamine (Et3N), chitosan, OTC, N,N-dimethyllformamide (DMF), ethylene glycol, N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP), trimesoylchloride (TMC) and acetic anhydride were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai,China). All reagents were commercially obtained and used without further purification.

Chitosan and acetic acid were added to 50 ml autoclave, which was then heated to 160℃. After 24 h, the suspension was obtained by natural cooling. Then the suspension was put into the centrifuge tube, which was centrifuged at 12,000 RPM for 5 min to remove big particles. Followingly, the suspension was dried under vacuum at 90℃ to obtain CQDs.

ODA (7.5 mmol) and NMP(80 ml) were put into 250 ml three-necked round bottom flask and stirred at room temperature for 30 min. CQDs was put and kept stirring for 30 min. Then BPDA(8.0 mmol) was added in and stirred to complete dissolution. Followingly, acetic anhydride (11.07 ml) was put with continuous stirring for 5 min and then Et3N(2.04 ml) was added. 15 min later,the solution of NMP (10 ml) with TMC (0.24 mmol) was poured and stirred for 5 min. The mixture was transferred to a beaker and the gel occurred in 30 min. The solvent within the gels was exchanged with ethanol 7 times (for 24 h each time). Then, the wet gels were dried by supercritical CO2 for 6 h at 8.2 MPa and 40℃. Finally, PI/CQDs aerogels were prepared.
The products containing CQDs of 0.5, 1.0, and 3.0 wt% were denoted by PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1, and PI/CQDs-3, respectively. Pure polyimide aerogel was also prepared and was denoted by PI.

Typically, aerogel samples (0.1 g) were put into OTC solution (100.0 ml, 10.0 mg/L). The mixtures were in darkness for 1 h with continuous stirring to establish adsorption/desorption equilibrium before visible-light illumination. The photocatalytic degradation was measured by a 250 W mercury lamp with a UV cutoff filter (λ ≥ 420 nm). 1.0 ml solution were filtered by 0.22 μm Millipore filters at pre-determined time intervals to remove the photocatalyst. The degradation efficiency was obtained through measuring the absorbance of OTC at 364 nm wavelength by a UV–vis spectrometer. The degradation rates of the photocatalysts were calculated by C/C0, where C0 signify the starting concentration of OTC solution in adsorption/desorption balance and C represent residual concentration after irradiation, respectively.

The photocatalysts were then collected and washed with ultrapure water and ethanol several times for next use. Three cycles of photocatalytic degradation for OTC were conducted to measure the cycling stability of photocatalysts.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with the tube voltage of 40 kV and current of 40 mA. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra was obtained on a Nicolet NEXUS870 spectrometer using the KBr pellet support. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was measured by a Hitachi S4800 FE-SEM system. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was achieved by PHI 5000 Versa Probe X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. UV–vis spectra were performed with a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra was collected by a Horiba Scientific FluoroMax-4 fluorometer spectrometer. Fluorescence emission spectrum was performed with Horiba Jobin Yvon fluorescence spectroscope by time-correlated single-photon counting. Brunner-Emmet-Teller (BET) specific surface area was obtained by N2 adsorption–desorption isothermals on Micromeritics ASAP 2020. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed in a standard three-electrode cell system with a CHI760D electrochemical workstation (CH Instrument Company, Shanghai, China) and carried out at open circuit potential with an AC amplitude of 10 mV from 100 Hz to 1 MHz in a 0.2 M Na2SO4 solution. Namely, Pt wire and Ag/AgCl were used as counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively.

Fig. 1 showed the FTIR spectra of PI, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels. The peaks at 1773 cm−1 and 1713 cm−1 in the spectra were ascribed to C = O stretching vibration occurring in the imide ring, respectively. The peak at 1500 cm−1 was ascribed to stretching vibration of benzene ring, 1373 cm−1 was attributed to C-N stretching vibration, and 1240 cm−1 was owing to C-O-C vibration. According to the figure, the peak intensity of 1773 cm−1, 1713 cm−1 and 1240 cm−1 of PI/CQDs aerogel increased compared to that of pure polyimide aerogel. This is because CQDs had COOH and C-O-C groups, whose peaks were overlapped with those vibration peaks from the polyimide aerogel.

Fig. 2 displayed XRD spectra of PI, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels. According to the figure, two typical wide diffraction peaks at 15° and 24° occurred in the curves of PI, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels. There is no sharp diffraction peak in PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3, showing that the crystal structure of PI/CQDs aerogels is amorphous. It is noted that the intensity of the peak at 24° of PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels increased compared to that of PI. This is because that this peak was also corresponding to the structure of CQDs. The peaks at 24° of polyimide aerogel and CQDs were overlapped.

Fig. 3 showed SEM images of (a) PI, (b) PI/CQDs-0.5, (c) PI/CQDs-1 and (d) PI/CQDs-3 aerogels. According to SEM images, all of the PI/CQDs aerogels displayed porous structure, which was similar to that of PI aerogels. The CQDs, which was covered on the polyimide aerogels, occurred in the SEM images of PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels. Owing to strong quantum confinement and edge effects, CQDs could be anchored intimately on the polyimide aerogel by π -π stacking interactions [23]. The porous structure was conductive to adsorb organic pollutant. Meanwhile, more active sites could occur on the surface of the polyimide aerogel, which favored their interaction with organic pollutant.

Fig. 4 showed the BET result of PI, PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels. According to the Fig. 4, hysteresis loops occurred on the curves of PI, PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels, showing that the structures of PI and PI/CQDs aerogels were mesoporous. It is found that PI/CQDs-1 had the largest hysteresis loop, which moved up. The phenomenon showed that PI/CQDs-1 had largest pore volume and porous structure [32]. It is known that mesoporous structure could facilitate mass transfer and the separation of photogenerated electron and hole [65]. BET surface area of PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels were 351 m2/g, 368 m2/g and 304 m2/g, which were higher than 249 m2/g of PI aerogel. The large surface area favored the PI/CQDs aerogels to absorb the small molecules and benefited the charge transfer. Moreover, more active sites existed on the surface of the polyimide aerogels, which facilitated their interaction with the absorbed molecules. Thus, the large surface area was very beneficial for the photocatalysis. Notably, PI/CQDs-1 had largest surface area. Consequently, PI/CQDs-1 was expected to have the best photocatalytic activity.

XPS was an effective method to measure the change of the atom chemical state and thus study the interaction among the atoms. Fig. 5 showed XPS spectra of N 1 s and C 1 s of PI, PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels. According to Fig. 5(a), the peak of N atom at about 400.12 ev in the PI curve were corresponding to N atom in the imide of the polyimide. The peaks of N atom at this position were shifted up in the curves of PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels, indicating that the chemical state of some N atoms in the PI/CQDs aerogels were changed. According to Fig. 5(b), the C 1 s peak in the polyimide was divided into two peaks at 284.79ev and 288.21ev, which were attributed to adventitious carbon and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, respectively. The peaks at 288.21ev moved up in the PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels, implying that the chemical state of C atoms was also influenced by the CQDs. The result above confirmed the interaction between polyimide and CQDs occurred. The interaction could arise from π-π stacking structures [66,67].

Fig. 6 showed VBXPS(a), Kubelka-Munk plots(b), schematic band structures(c) of PI, PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels. According to VBXPS(a), the VB edges of PI, PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels were 1.76ev, 2.00ev, 2.06ev and 1.93ev, respectively, displaying that the addition of the CQDs increased the VB edges of the polyimide aerogels. According to Kubelka-Munk plots (b), the band gaps of PI, PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels were 2.71ev, 2.50ev, 2.48ev and 2.58ev, respectively. It is noted that PI/CQDs aerogels possessed narrower band gaps than PI aerogel. This is because that PI/CQDs aerogels could form a heterojunction, which leaded to a significant reduction in the band gap. The photoexcited electrons could more effectively migrate from PI aerogels to CQDs via well heterojunction interface and retard their recombination with holes. The schematic band structures of PI, PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels were obtained. The narrower band gaps could enhance the light response range for the semiconductor and the more positive VB edges of the semiconductor had stronger oxidation property [45,68].Thus PI/CQDs aerogels were expected to have better photocatalytic property than PI aerogel.
Fig. 7 showed UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of PI, PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels. According to Fig. 7, the intrinsic absorption

edges of PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 had obvious redshift compared to that of PI, showing that the CQDs influenced the optical property of the PI aerogel. The spectra intensity between 380 nm and 600 nm of PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels was stronger than that of polyimide aerogels. This result displayed that the CQDs could increase the visible light absorption. This is attributed to the upconversion of CQDs, which could absorb the visible light with longer wavelength and emit the ultroviolet or visible light with shorter wavelength. Thus the visible light utilization of polyimide aerogel was improved. In addition, owing to the characteristics of polymer semiconductors, the polymer chain had strong electron coupling. The charge could be transferred freely in a single molecular chain. So the charge transfer among polymer chains is the critical step to influence charge transfer rate [69]. The CQDs could act as the medium among the polymer chains which was beneficial to the charge transfer among the polymer chains. Especially, when the content of CQDs was 1%, PI/CQDs-1 had the strongest light absorption. The good light absorption could favor the photocatalytic activity.

The charge separation and recombination on the material surface can be studied by fluorescence spectroscopy. Under the same excitation wavelength, the low photoluminescence intensity stands for the efficient charge transfer. Fig. 8 showed the photoluminescence spectra of PI, PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels. According to the figure, the fluorescence intensity of PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels decreased compared to that of pure polyimide aerogel. The result showed that the CQDs could increase the efficient charge transfer, which was also helpful to the photocatalytic activity. This is because that the good conductivity of CQDs favored the transfer and separation of the charge. In addition, CQDs could possibly enhance electron π - π coupling among the conjuncted polyimide chains, which could in turn supply an efficient way for the charge transfer between the adjacent polyimide chains [70]. It is noted that PI/CQDs-1 possessed the lowest fluorescence intensity and thus the fastest charge transfer. When the content of the CQDs increased, the fluorescence intensity decreased, showing that the CQDs quantity could influence the charge transfer.

The EIS measurement was further used to investigate the efficiency of separation and transfer of charge carriers. Fig. 9 describes the Nyquist plots of PI and PI/CQDs-1 aerogels. A smaller semicircle radius revealed lower charge transfer resistance and higher charge transfer efficiency [60]. As can be seen from Fig. 9, the semicircle radius for PI/CQD-1 was smaller than that for pure PI, implying that PI/CQD-1 possessed higher efficiency for separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers.

Fig. 10 (a) showed photocatalytic degradation curves of OTC over PI, PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels. According to the degradation curves, PI, PI/CQDs-0.5, PI/CQDs-1 and PI/CQDs-3 aerogels had good photocatalytic property. The photocatalytic property of PI/CQDs aerogel was stronger than that of PI aerogels, showing that the CQDs could improve the photocatalytic activity of the polyimide aerogels. It is reasonable that the upconversion of CQDs could transfer visible light to ultraviolet light and increase the light harvesting capability of the PI/CQDs aerogels [22]. Meanwhile, the CQDs had good conductivity and could accept the generated electrons from polyimide aerogel, thus increased the photoconductive response [23]. The characteristics facilitated the generation and separation of photogenerated electron and holes, which prolonged the lifetime of the charge carrier and favored the photocatalysis. It is noted that PI/CQDs-1 had the highest photocatalytic rate and displayed the best photocatalytic performance. As the increase of the CQDs, the photocatalytic rate of PI aerogels decreased. This is because the excessive CQDs could have the “shielding effect”, which decreased the light absorption and encapsulated the active sites of the polyimide aerogel. In addition to the photocatalytic activity, the stability is another important characteristic to evaluate a catalyst. So the stability and recyclability of PI/CQDs-1 were measured by repeating three cycles of OTC degradation. According to Fig. 10(b), there was no obvious change on the degradation rate after three consecutive cycles. It could be seen from Fig. 10(c) that there is no notable change in the XRD pattern of PI/CQDs-1 before and after the recycling photocatalytic tests. These results confirmed that the photocatalyst had good structural stability. The stability and recyclability of the aerogel photocatalyst were related to structural stability and mechanical property. Due to excellent stability and mechanical strength, the morphology and 3D network of the aerogel could be kept. The aggregation and the loss of active photocatalysts could be efficiently inhibited. Thus, the aerogel photocatalyst had good cycling degradation rate.

Aiming to do the research on photocatalytic mechanism, visible-light photocatalytic degradation measurements over OTC of PI/CQDs-1 aerogel with and without scavengers were conducted. During the process of the photocatalytic degradation, three active species, h+, ·O2 – and ·OH, could be able to oxide the pollutant. Three corresponding scavengers, EDTA, benzoquinone and methanol, were added to measure the photocatalytic degradation of OTC over PI/CQDs-1, respectively. Fig. 11 displayed visible-light photocatalytic degradation curves of OTC over PI/CQDs-1 aerogel with and without scavengers. According to the figure, the suppressed degradation rate over OTC of the PI/CQDs-1 was in order: EDTA > benzoquinone > methanol, showing the effect on the degradation of the species was in order: h+ > ·O2 – > ·OH. In addition, the photocatalytic degradation curves with methanol changes little compared to the curves without scavengers. Thus ·OH had little effect on the oxidizing the OTC.

We successfully prepared porous PI/CQDs aerogel photocatalyst. The specific surface area of the aerogel was up to 368 m2/g. Due to the upconversion and semiconductor characteristics, CQDs increased the visible absorption and speeded the charge transfer rate. The PI/CQDs aerogel showed better photocatalytic activity than PI aerogel under the visible light. The h+ and ·O2 – played important roles to the photocatalytic degradation. The study supplied an efficient method to design and prepare the photocatalyst.

Yujie Bai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xibin Yi: Software, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Bing Li: . Shiwei Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Zijie Fan: Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors are thankful for the supports of the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province(Grant No. ZR2019BEM025); Young Doctor Cooperation Foundation of Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences) (Grant No. 2019BSHZ0016); College Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (Grant No. S201910431075; xj201810431071); Science-Education-Industry Integration Innovation Pilot Project of Qilu University of Technology(Grant No. 2020KJC-GH13)
We declare that the data supporting the findings of this study are available within the article.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-based metal-free catalysts for key reactions involved in energy conversion and storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhao, D. Wang, R. Amal, L. Dai, Carbon-based metal-free catalysts for key reactions involved in energy conversion and storage, Adv. Mater., 31 (2019) 1801526.
S. Zhiping, Z. Hui, Z. Yunhong, Polyimides: promising energy-storage materials, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed., 49 (2010) 8444-8448.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polyimide aerogels cross-linked through amine functionalized polyoligomeric silsesquioxane

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.Q. Guo, M.A.B. Meador, L. McCorkle, D.J. Quade, J.A. Guo, B. Hamilton, M. Cakmak, G. Sprowl, Polyimide aerogels cross-linked through amine functionalized polyoligomeric silsesquioxane, ACS Appl. Mat. Inter., 3 (2011) 546-552.
J. Guo, B.N. Nguyen, L.C. Li, M.A.B. Meador, D.A. Scheiman, M. Cakmak, Clay reinforced polyimide/silica hybrid aerogel, J. Mater. Chem. A, 1 (2013) 7211-7221.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of N, C Codoped hierarchical porous microsphere ZnS as a aisible light-responsive photocatalyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Muruganandham, Y. Kusumoto, Synthesis of N, C Codoped hierarchical porous microsphere ZnS as a aisible light-responsive photocatalyst, J. Phys. Chem. C, 113 (2009) 16144-16150.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.T. Li, X.D. He, Z.H. Kang, H. Huang, Y. Liu, J.L. Liu, S.Y. Lian, C.H.A. Tsang, X.B. Yang, S.T. Lee, Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design, Angew. Chem.Int. Edit., 49 (2010) 4430-4434.
